one_line_takeaway: A 2024 survey that organizes ~45 recommender-system poisoning attacks into a novel strategy/goal/capability taxonomy and ships ARLib, an open-source library of 15+ attack implementations, to standardize comparison.
key_finding: As a survey the paper reports no experimental attack-success numbers in its body. Its quantitative facts are: ~45 papers reviewed; a 3-category, 9-subtype taxonomy; and ARLib integrating 15+ PAR methods. It compared 15 PAR models via ARLib but publishes those findings on GitHub, not in the paper. It contrasts ARLib with the prior RecAD/ADRec library (6 methods, all pre-2021) and names five future directions (novel victim contexts, sophisticated intents, PAR theory, long-term impact, neutralization).
core_contribution: A survey of poisoning attacks against recommender systems (abbreviated PAR by the authors) that proposes a new three-way taxonomy — Component-Specific, Goal-Driven, and Capability Probing attacks — and releases an accompanying open-source library, ARLib. The paper's own contributions are explicitly threefold: (1) a novel taxonomy organizing existing PAR literature by attack strategy, objective, and attacker capability; (2) a review of ~45 recent papers with a discussion of open challenges and future directions; and (3) ARLib, a library integrating 15+ PAR methods and standard datasets to standardize empirical comparison. Note this is a survey: the individual attack methods discussed (e.g., GraphAttack, PoisonRec, CopyAttack, LOKI, PipAttack, FedRecAttack, AUSH, TrialAttack, ModelExtractionAttack) are prior works it cites and categorizes, NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Attacker injects malicious data D_M — typically fake user profiles (controlled malicious users U_M engaging in strategic interactions) and sometimes modified item features — into the training data of a victim recommender to manipulate its learned representations and outputs. Objectives span untargeted system degradation (harming overall recommendation quality / user experience, e.g., inter-platform competition) and targeted manipulation (promoting/demoting specific items for chosen user groups, e.g., e-commerce or misinformation promotion), plus hybrid goals. The taxonomy's Capability-Probing branch explicitly models realistic attacker constraints: knowledge (white/gray/black-box access to the victim model and data, often forcing surrogate models), cost/budget (limited number of injectable malicious users/interactions, down to single-user injection), and invisibility (evading deployed shilling/fake-user detectors, e.g., via GAN-aligned profiles). Formalized as bi-level optimization where the attacker's outer objective is maximized subject to the recommender's training as the inner constraint.
limitations: It is a taxonomy/survey without independent quantitative evaluation — the "significant findings" from comparing 15 models via ARLib are deferred to an external GitHub file and not substantiated with numbers or tables in the paper, so claims of the library's usefulness cannot be verified from the paper alone. No specific benchmark datasets, metrics, or head-to-head results are given in the text. Coverage is a curated ~45 papers up to early 2024 (arXiv v3, Jan 2024); LLM-based and multimodal recommender attacks are acknowledged as underexplored rather than covered in depth. The taxonomy's three branches are, by the authors' own admission, interconnected and overlapping rather than mutually exclusive, so a given method can belong to multiple categories. Defense mechanisms are largely out of scope (the survey is attack-centric).

datasets_used: None reported: this is a survey and runs no experiments in the paper; it states ARLib incorporates widely used PAR datasets but names no specific dataset in the text
quantitative_results: Taxonomy of 3 top-level categories (Component-Specific, Goal-Driven, Capability Probing) with 9 sub-types
ARLib integrates 15+ PAR methods (stated as 'over 15' / '15 influential')
Compared 15 PAR models via ARLib, with findings hosted externally on GitHub rather than in the paper
PAR formalized as a bi-level optimization (Eq. 2), specialized into Eqs. 3-5
Contrasted against prior library RecAD/ADRec: 6 PAR methods, all pre-2021
No attack-success-rate, hit-ratio, or accuracy numbers are reported in the paper body
baselines_compared: Not applicable — survey paper with no experimental baseline comparison; only a qualitative comparison of ARLib against the prior RecAD/ADRec library (6 methods, pre-2021) and against prior PAR surveys (Si and Li 2020; Deldjoo et al. 2021; Fan et al. 2022 trustworthy-RS; Anelli et al. 2021 robust-RS)
